metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how the `pypi` environment's deployment branch/tag policy must be updated (e.g., add `main` or allow `workflow_dispatch`) so that the dispatch-from-main publish job can deploy to the `pypi` environment, and update AC7 to include this as part of the operator-attested environment configuration.
findings.finding: The `pypi` deployment environment is currently restricted to `v*` tag refs (`docs/releasing.md:25`: 'GitHub environment `pypi` exists, restricted to `v*` tags'). Under the new design, the `publish` job runs in a `workflow_dispatch`-triggered workflow context; GitHub evaluates deployment branch/tag policies against the triggering ref, not a tag the workflow itself creates mid-run. A `workflow_dispatch` ref is `refs/heads/main`, not a `v*` tag, so the `pypi` environment gate will block publication on every dispatch-from-main run — undermining the primary control.
findings.location: What section / 'Human authorization — required-reviewers environment' bullet; docs/releasing.md line 25; AC7
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope section and AC7, explicitly state that the `pypi` environment's deployment branch policy must be widened from 'restricted to v* tags only' to also permit deployment from `refs/heads/main` (or from `workflow_dispatch` triggered by main). Alternatively, restructure so that the tag-push fallback — now pointing at a workflow-created tag — is the sole deployment trigger, and the dispatch path ends after creating the tag and kicking off the tag-triggered run.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (and corresponding enforcement in the workflow) that explicitly rejects a `workflow_dispatch` run targeting a non-`main` ref — e.g., a job-level `if: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main'` condition or a fail-fast step — and verify it by a test/run that dispatches from a non-main ref and observes rejection before any build step.
findings.finding: The plan's PRIMARY CONTROL is 'release initiated from the `main` ref via `workflow_dispatch`,' yet no AC verifies the workflow ENFORCES this constraint — i.e., that dispatching from a non-main ref is detected and rejected before any build/publish step. Without an enforcement check (e.g., a job-level `if: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main'` guard or an explicit fail step) and a corresponding AC that exercises the wrong-ref path, the dispatch-from-main guarantee is documentation-only, not machine-enforced.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC #1 / What section — 'Primary control — initiate from `main` via `workflow_dispatch`'
findings.suggested_fix: In the Acceptance Criteria, add: '- [ ] A `workflow_dispatch` run targeting any ref other than `refs/heads/main` is rejected before any build/publish job starts — verified by dispatching from a feature branch and observing the workflow fail with a message naming the forbidden ref.' In `release.yml`, add a guard on the `version-check` job: `if: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main'` (or an explicit `run: if [ "$GITHUB_REF" != "refs/heads/main" ]; then echo "Must dispatch from main"; exit 1; fi` step).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Address the `pypi` environment deployment branch/tag policy: either update it to also allow `refs/heads/main` (or remove the `v*` tag restriction) so `workflow_dispatch`-triggered runs can deploy to the environment, or explicitly document that the policy must be changed as part of this story's operator steps.
findings.finding: The `pypi` GitHub deployment environment is currently 'restricted to `v*` tags' (`docs/releasing.md:25`). GitHub evaluates environment deployment branch/tag policies against the *triggering* workflow ref, not a tag the workflow creates mid-run. A `workflow_dispatch` initiated from `refs/heads/main` does not satisfy a `v*` tag restriction, so the `publish` job's attempt to deploy to the `pypi` environment will be blocked by GitHub's own gate on every dispatch-initiated release — the primary control will silently fail at the environment gate.
findings.location: What section ('Human authorization — required-reviewers environment' bullet); AC7 ('operator-attested'); docs/releasing.md line 25
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope section and/or AC7, add an explicit step: update the `pypi` GitHub environment's deployment branch/tag policy to allow `refs/heads/main` (in addition to or instead of `refs/tags/v*`), so the dispatch-from-main path can reach the required-reviewers gate. Document this in `docs/releasing.md` under prerequisites.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Remove or correct the `infra/terraform-github/main.tf` entry in the leaf ticket's `file_impact` metadata: the plan explicitly delivers no change to that file, and the reason 'tag ruleset restricting v* writes' contradicts both the leaf plan text and the parent epic's scope for Finding 1.
findings.finding: The leaf ticket's `file_impact` metadata registers `infra/terraform-github/main.tf` with reason 'tag ruleset restricting v* writes', but the leaf plan body explicitly states 'infra/terraform-github/main.tf — no change'. This contradiction adds a deliverable (tag-push restriction) that the parent epic does not include in Finding 1's scope and actively treats as a resolved non-action — making the metadata diverge from both the leaf plan text and the parent contract.
findings.location: Leaf ticket fd84-ba00-ba00-4f73 `file_impact` metadata entry for `infra/terraform-github/main.tf`
findings.suggested_fix: Update the leaf's file_impact entry for infra/terraform-github/main.tf: either delete it entirely (since the plan delivers no change there) or replace the reason with 'no change — deliberate no-tag-lock decision unchanged' to accurately reflect the plan's intent.

findings.finding: The leaf ticket's `file_impact` metadata entry for `infra/terraform-github/main.tf` carries the reason 'tag ruleset restricting v* writes', which directly contradicts the leaf's own plan text ('infra/terraform-github/main.tf — **no change**') and introduces a deliverable the parent's plan does not contain. The parent epic describes `main.tf:23-28` tags as 'deliberately left unprotected' and Finding 1 is scoped to the ancestry/version gate — not a tag-push ruleset. An implementer following the `file_impact` metadata would write a Terraform tag ruleset that is out of scope for both the leaf and the parent.
findings.location: Leaf ticket `file_impact` metadata / `infra/terraform-github/main.tf` entry
findings.suggested_fix: Remove the `infra/terraform-github/main.tf` entry from `file_impact` entirely (it is a no-change file), or replace the reason with 'no change — tag-lock deliberately out of scope per plan' to make the metadata self-consistent and aligned with the parent.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate in the Scope section that the `if:` conditions on `publish`, `github_release`, and `mcp_registry` jobs (release.yml lines 53, 81, 116) must be updated to also trigger on the `workflow_dispatch` event path (e.g. when the workflow-created tag ref is present, or via an explicit `github.event_name == 'workflow_dispatch'` branch).
findings.finding: The plan makes `workflow_dispatch` the primary publication trigger, but the three downstream jobs `publish`, `github_release`, and `mcp_registry` all gate on `if: github.event_name == 'push' && startsWith(github.ref, 'refs/tags/v')` (lines 53, 81, 116). The Scope section does not enumerate updating these `if:` conditions as required edits, so the plan's primary control path would silently skip all publication steps if implemented as-described.
findings.location: Scope section / `.github/workflows/release.yml` lines 53, 81, 116

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify how the tag-push fallback path is prevented from firing when the dispatch workflow itself creates the v* tag (e.g., add a `github.event_name == 'workflow_dispatch'` short-circuit, or document that the tag-push path guards against re-entrancy with a condition).
findings.finding: When workflow_dispatch creates the v* tag (described in 'What' and AC3), the retained v*-tag-push fallback trigger will ALSO fire on that same tag — making the 'fallback' a co-trigger that runs the tag-push path on every dispatch-initiated release, not only on stray manual tag pushes. The plan does not address this re-entrancy or explain how the two paths are disambiguated (e.g., a condition on `github.event_name`, or suppressing the tag trigger when the dispatch already ran).
findings.location: What section ('The workflow creates the v* tag after checks pass' + 'Keep the existing v* tag-push trigger as a fallback') vs AC3 and AC4
findings.suggested_fix: Add a condition to the tag-push-triggered jobs (or the fallback check step) that skips/exits when `github.actor` matches the bot/workflow identity that created the tag, OR use `if: github.event_name == 'push'` only when the tag was NOT created by the dispatch workflow (e.g., check for a specific tag annotation or a workflow-set env variable). Alternatively, state explicitly that the dispatch workflow will NOT push a real git tag but instead use the GitHub Releases API or create the tag only inside the release job with a condition that prevents the tag-push trigger from being recognized.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a proving procedure for AC2 and AC4 that specifies how to trigger the test runs safely (e.g., 'push a test branch with a deliberate version mismatch and run `workflow_dispatch` against it; for AC4, push a test tag pointing at a commit not reachable from `origin/main` on a fork or under a `test/` tag namespace, then delete it after verification').
findings.finding: AC2 and AC4 require concrete CI test runs (a deliberate-mismatch run and a non-ancestor-tag run) to prove the new workflow controls work, but the plan specifies no safe mechanism for executing those runs — no test-branch strategy, no guidance on creating a non-ancestor tag safely, and no statement of where these runs happen (feature branch, dry-run job, etc.).
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2 ('verified by a run where the values deliberately disagree') and AC4 ('verified by a tag on a non-ancestor commit failing... and a tag on a non-tip ancestor passing')

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the default version-derivation rule for the tag-push fallback path (e.g., 'strip `refs/tags/v` from `GITHUB_REF` to obtain the version value passed to the version-check job') so the executor does not have to make a silent design choice.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify how `version_input` is derived in the `v*` tag-push fallback path. In the `workflow_dispatch` path the version comes from an explicit user input; in the tag-push path there is no such input, so the version-check job cannot run 'the same checks' without a design decision the plan defers to the executor.
findings.location: What section — 'Defense-in-depth — ancestry assertion' bullet; Scope — 'keep the `v*` tag trigger as a gated fallback'
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the 'Defense-in-depth' bullet: 'On tag push, the version-check job derives `version_input` by stripping the `refs/tags/v` prefix from `GITHUB_REF`, then runs the same equality assertion against `pyproject.toml` and `server.json`.' This makes the fallback path's version-check behaviour unambiguous.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the version-check job specification how `version_input` is resolved on the tag-push fallback path (e.g., 'derive from `github.ref_name` by stripping the `v` prefix when `inputs.version` is empty'), so both trigger paths produce a non-empty, comparable version string for the lockstep assertion.
findings.finding: The version-check job asserts `version_input == pyproject.toml version == server.json version` but the plan does not specify how `version_input` is resolved on the tag-push fallback path, where no `workflow_dispatch` input is provided. The plan says 'The GitHub-Release and MCP jobs derive the version from the dispatch input / the tag the workflow just created' using a slash ('or') that implies an ambiguous dual resolution strategy, but the version-check job definition doesn't clearly address what `version_input` contains when the trigger is a tag push.
findings.location: What section / 'Version-lockstep, before build' bullet; AC2; Scope / release.yml description
findings.suggested_fix: Add a step that sets VERSION=$([ -n "${{ inputs.version }}" ] && echo "${{ inputs.version }}" || echo "${GITHUB_REF_NAME#v}") before the lockstep assertion, ensuring both dispatch and tag-push paths resolve a non-empty version string.

findings.finding: The evidence-recording deliverable ('Record the approved SHA, tag, manifest versions, and artifact hashes in release evidence (e.g. a job summary / uploaded evidence file)') has no co-located schema specifying the required fields, format, or storage mechanism, and no Acceptance Criterion verifies its existence or correctness. This is a referenced output format whose value vocabulary is never defined.
findings.location: What section — final bullet: 'Record the approved SHA, tag, manifest versions, and artifact hashes in release evidence (e.g. a job summary / uploaded evidence file)'; Acceptance Criteria — no corresponding AC

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion for the release-evidence deliverable (job summary / uploaded evidence file recording SHA, tag, manifest versions, and artifact hashes), or explicitly mark it as a nice-to-have / out-of-scope so implementers and reviewers have a clear signal.
findings.finding: The deliverable 'Record the approved SHA, tag, manifest versions, and artifact hashes in release evidence (a job summary / uploaded evidence file)' is described in the 'What' section but has no corresponding acceptance criterion — it is an orphaned deliverable.
findings.location: What section, last bullet: 'Record the approved SHA, tag, manifest versions, and artifact hashes in release evidence (a job summary / uploaded evidence file)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC such as: 'The workflow uploads a release-evidence artifact (or writes a job summary) containing the approved SHA, tag, manifest versions, and artifact hashes — verified by inspecting the Actions run for a dispatch-initiated release.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Codify the `pypi` GitHub environment and its required-reviewers policy as a `github_repository_environment` (+ `github_repository_environment_deployment_policy` or `reviewers` block) Terraform resource in `infra/terraform-github/main.tf`, so the load-bearing approval gate is reproducible as-code and covered by drift detection — OR explicitly document in the plan why operator-attestation is the accepted posture and what compensating controls exist.
findings.finding: The `pypi` GitHub Actions environment's required-reviewers setting — described as one of the two 'load-bearing controls' for production publication safety — is managed entirely out-of-band (manual GitHub UI) and operator-attested only via a comment or screenshot (AC line 7). There is no `github_repository_environment` + reviewer-policy Terraform resource in `infra/terraform-github/` that codifies and enforces it. An accidental UI change, a repo migration, or a future GitHub org restructure would silently remove the approval gate with no IaC drift detection.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC line 7 (operator-attested); Scope section ('pypi' environment); infra/terraform-github/main.tf (no github_repository_environment resource present)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add explicit error handling for the `git fetch origin main` step in the ancestry assertion — ensure fetch failures emit a distinct, named error message (not silently swallowed or conflated with ancestry violations) and hard-fail the job.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify error handling or observability for the `git fetch origin main` + `git merge-base --is-ancestor` ancestry check in the tag-push fallback path — if the fetch fails (network error, permission issue), the job's failure mode (silent skip vs. hard fail vs. logged error) is unspecified, and there is no mention of surfacing this failure distinctly from an ancestry violation.
findings.location: What section — 'Defense-in-depth — ancestry assertion'; AC4
findings.suggested_fix: Split the ancestry check into two steps: (1) `git fetch origin main` with a clear `|| { echo 'ERROR: failed to fetch origin/main — cannot verify ancestry'; exit 1; }` guard; (2) `git merge-base --is-ancestor "$GITHUB_SHA" origin/main || { echo 'ERROR: SHA is not an ancestor of main'; exit 1; }`. This ensures each failure mode is named and observable.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add input validation in the `version-check` job that: (a) asserts the version input matches the expected semver pattern (e.g., `^[0-9]+\.[0-9]+\.[0-9]+` with optional pre-release suffix), (b) checks that the `v<version>` tag does not already exist on the remote, and (c) emits an actionable error message for each failure case; update AC 2 (or add an AC) to cover these rejection scenarios.
findings.finding: The plan does not address what happens when the version input provided at dispatch time is malformed (e.g., empty string, non-semver, or a version that already has a published tag) — these error states for the user-facing version input are absent.
findings.location: What — Primary control bullet ('a version input'); Acceptance Criteria — AC 1 and AC 2

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add permalink citations (GitHub workflow file URLs + line ranges, or specific release tag) for both the uv release workflow and pydantic pre-build version-check patterns cited in the 'What' section, so the attributed designs can be independently verified by implementers and reviewers.
findings.finding: The plan explicitly cites uv's release process and pydantic's pre-build version check as the specific prior-art patterns being adopted ('Adopt uv's proven release process and pydantic's pre-build version check', 'pydantic pattern', 'uv, and the finding's own remediation'), but provides no links, workflow-file URLs, or specific version/tag citations for these patterns. An implementer cannot confirm whether uv actually implements workflow_dispatch + required-reviewers environment in the described shape, or whether pydantic's version check is truly a pre-build step (rather than in-build or post-build), meaning the prior-art anchors are unverifiable as written.
findings.location: What section — 'Adopt uv's proven release process and pydantic's pre-build version check'; 'pydantic pattern' bullet; '(uv, and the finding's own remediation)' parenthetical
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'What' section, add inline citations such as: 'uv's proven release process (astral-sh/uv/.github/workflows/release.yml, `workflow_dispatch` trigger + `publish` environment with required reviewers)' and 'pydantic's pre-build version check (pydantic/pydantic/.github/workflows/ci.yml or release.yml, the check_version step)' with pinned commit/tag URLs. If the exact files differ from this suggestion, use the actual permalinks.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare the integrity-verification posture for the mcp-publisher binary in the plan: either pin to a specific release version + verify a recorded SHA-256 before execution (e.g. `echo "<sha256>  mcp-publisher" | sha256sum -c`), or document a rationale for why the floating reference is acceptable given the id-token:write credential it holds.
findings.finding: The mcp_registry job (which holds id-token:write for MCP Registry publication) downloads the mcp-publisher binary from a floating 'releases/latest' URL with no version pin and no SHA-256 integrity check before executing it. The plan explicitly lists release.yml as a modified deliverable and describes hardening the release pipeline, but does not declare how the integrity of this boundary-crossing binary is verified — leaving the OIDC credential exposed to a compromised or substituted binary.
findings.location: .github/workflows/release.yml:127-134 (mcp_registry job, 'Install mcp-publisher' step); plan 'Scope' section (release.yml is an explicit deliverable)
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'What' or 'Scope' section, specify: pin mcp-publisher to a specific tagged version (not 'latest') in the download URL and record the expected SHA-256 in a checked-in file or the workflow itself; add a `sha256sum -c` step before `./mcp-publisher login`. This mirrors the approach already used for other supply-chain controls in the plan (PEP 740 attestations, Trusted Publishing) and closes the binary-integrity gap on the id-token:write path.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare the access posture for the pypa/gh-action-pypi-publish action reference in the plan: either commit to SHA-pinning it (e.g. `pypa/gh-action-pypi-publish@<sha>`) as part of this story's release.yml edits, or explicitly document the rationale for retaining the mutable tag given that it holds id-token:write.
findings.finding: The publish job uses pypa/gh-action-pypi-publish@release/v1 — a mutable floating tag reference — while holding id-token:write for PyPI Trusted Publishing. The plan modifies the publish job (adding the required-reviewers pypi environment gate) but does not declare whether the action reference will be pinned to an immutable SHA or left as a mutable tag. An undeclared posture on a boundary-crossing path that holds a PyPI OIDC credential.
findings.location: .github/workflows/release.yml:65 (publish job, pypa/gh-action-pypi-publish@release/v1); plan 'Scope' section (release.yml is an explicit deliverable); plan AC line 'The workflow creates the v* tag after checks pass'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how to prevent the `push: tags: v*` fallback from co-firing when the dispatch workflow itself creates the tag (e.g., adding `if: github.event_name == 'push'` guards on fallback-only jobs, or documenting that workflow-created tags do not fire the push trigger in this repo's configuration).
findings.finding: When the `workflow_dispatch` path creates the `v*` tag (AC3 requires this), GitHub Actions will also fire the retained `push: tags: v*` trigger on that same tag — because a `git push` of a `refs/tags/v*` ref from within a workflow is platform-identical to a human push. The plan calls this trigger a 'fallback', but it will co-fire on every dispatch-initiated release, causing two concurrent workflow runs (one dispatch-path, one tag-push-path) to race for the same environment approval and publish slot.
findings.location: What section ('The workflow creates the v* tag after checks pass' + 'Keep the existing v* tag-push trigger as a fallback'); AC3; AC4
findings.suggested_fix: Add a condition `if: github.triggering_actor != 'github-actions[bot]'` or use the GitHub API to push the tag (API-created tags do not fire push triggers in some configurations) — or alternatively suppress the tag-push path entirely when a dispatch run is in progress using a concurrency group. Document the chosen disambiguation mechanism in the Scope section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a workflow step (or `if:` condition on the version-check job) that asserts `github.ref == 'refs/heads/main'` and fails with an explicit error when the dispatch is triggered from any other ref; add a corresponding AC verifying that a dispatch from a non-main ref is rejected before any build work begins.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify a guard that fails fast when `workflow_dispatch` is triggered from a ref other than `main` — the primary control ('reviewed-and-landed main by construction') is silently bypassed if an operator mistakenly selects a feature branch or tag ref in the GitHub UI dropdown.
findings.location: What — Primary control bullet; Acceptance Criteria — first AC item
findings.suggested_fix:
At the top of the `version-check` job add:
```yaml
- name: Assert dispatch from main
  if: github.event_name == 'workflow_dispatch'
  run: |
    if [ "${{ github.ref }}" != "refs/heads/main" ]; then
      echo "ERROR: workflow_dispatch must be run from main (got ${{ github.ref }})"
      exit 1
    fi
```
And update AC 1 to include: 'verified by a dispatch from a feature-branch ref being rejected before version-check completes.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/recovery procedure for the 'stranded tag' scenario (tag created but publish failed), specifying whether the tag should be deleted and the dispatch re-run, or whether the tag-push fallback can be used to resume publication.
findings.finding: The plan changes release initiation from a human-pushed v* tag to a dispatch-triggered workflow that auto-creates the tag after checks pass, but provides no rollback or back-out path for the 'stranded tag' failure mode: if the version-check passes and the workflow creates the v* tag but the subsequent publish step fails (e.g., pypi environment approval times out or is rejected), a v* tag exists in the repo pointing at main with no corresponding published release, and the plan gives no guidance on how to recover.
findings.location: What section ('The workflow creates the `v*` tag after checks pass') and AC3/AC7

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify an idempotency strategy for tag creation (e.g., check-then-skip if tag already points to the same SHA, or delete-and-recreate on retry) and define a cleanup/retire path for tags created during failed runs.
findings.finding: The tag-creation step is not idempotent: if the workflow creates the v* tag but a downstream job (build, publish, etc.) subsequently fails and the workflow is re-run, the tag-creation step will fail because the tag already exists — leaving the pipeline broken until someone manually deletes the tag.
findings.location: What section — 'The workflow creates the v* tag after checks pass'; Acceptance Criteria AC3
findings.suggested_fix: In the tag-creation step, add: `git tag v$VERSION $GITHUB_SHA 2>/dev/null || git tag -f v$VERSION $GITHUB_SHA` (idempotent if same SHA) or fail with a clear message if the existing tag points to a *different* SHA. Document in `docs/releasing.md` how to recover from a partially-failed release (manual `git tag -d` + `git push origin :refs/tags/vX.Y.Z` before retry).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update the `reconcile-bridge.yml:83-88` citation to accurately characterize its context (ticket-store merge-base, not release-ancestry), and add a direct justification for why `fetch-depth: 0` is required in the ancestry assertion step (e.g., cite `verify-identity.yaml:31` which uses `fetch-depth: 0` in a security-gate workflow, or explain it inline).
findings.finding: The plan cites `reconcile-bridge.yml:83-88` as the location where 'the repo documents this [that a shallow clone defeats ancestry]', but reading those actual lines shows they document `fetch-depth: 0` to prevent SNAPSHOT_INCONSISTENT corruption in ticket-store reconciliation — a wholly different context (merge-base for event-file compaction detection). The plan repurposes this citation to justify the `fetch-depth: 0` requirement for release ancestry checking, implying the codebase already has a precedent for this specific release-safety use case. The citation is contextually inaccurate for an E4 assertion-verification standpoint.
findings.location: What section — Defense-in-depth ancestry assertion paragraph: 'the repo documents this at reconcile-bridge.yml:83-88'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'the repo documents this at reconcile-bridge.yml:83-88' with 'a shallow clone truncates reachable-commit history, making `git merge-base --is-ancestor` exit non-zero for ancestors beyond the clone depth; `verify-identity.yaml` already uses `fetch-depth: 0` in a security gate (line 31) for the same reason'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `contents: write` to the explicit permission set for the tag-creation job in the Scope section, and note it must NOT be hoisted to the top-level workflow `permissions` block (to keep build/check jobs minimally privileged).
findings.finding: The plan's tag-creation step requires `contents: write` on the creating job, but the current `release.yml` declares `permissions: contents: read` at the workflow top level (line 23-25). The Scope section does not enumerate updating this permission as a required edit, leaving implementers to discover it at execution time.
findings.location: Scope section / `.github/workflows/release.yml` lines 23-25 and the new tag-creation step
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope bullet for `.github/workflows/release.yml`, add: 'grant `contents: write` only on the job that creates the tag (not at the workflow top level), so build/version-check jobs retain `contents: read`'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the tag-creation step's idempotency behavior: either use `git tag -f` / `git push origin refs/tags/vX.Y.Z --force` (with a rationale), or check for an existing tag and skip creation if it matches the expected version, so that a workflow re-run after a downstream failure can complete publication without manual tag cleanup.
findings.finding: The tag-creation step ('The workflow creates the v* tag after checks pass') is not idempotent: if the tag is created successfully but a downstream job (build, publish, github_release, mcp_registry) fails and the workflow is re-run, the tag-creation step will error ('tag already exists') and the entire workflow aborts before any publication can be retried. The plan has no specified behavior for this failure mode.
findings.location: What section / 'The workflow creates the v* tag after checks pass' bullet; AC3

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
